problems.title: SYCL Program Structure and dpcpp
problems.instructions:
SYCL programs are C++ and are compiled with the DPC++ compiler (**dpcpp**). They use the **sycl** namespace.

In this problem, write a minimal program that:
1. Includes `<sycl/sycl.hpp>`
2. Includes `<cstdio>`
3. Defines **main()** and prints exactly:

```
Hello from SYCL!
```

Then return 0.

{hints: You can use printf from cstdio for host-side output. You do not need to create a queue for this first problem.}

problems.title: The default_selector and Queue
problems.instructions:
SYCL uses a **queue** to submit work to a device. Create a queue with **default_selector_v**, then print exactly:

```
SYCL queue created
```

{hints: sycl::queue q(sycl::default_selector_v); then printf.}

problems.title: Submitting a single_task
problems.instructions:
Work is submitted via **submit()**. A **single_task** runs once (one work item). After **q.wait()**, print exactly:

```
Single task submitted
```

{hints: q.submit([&](sycl::handler& h) { h.single_task([=]() {}); }); q.wait(); printf.}

problems.title: parallel_for with range
problems.instructions:
**parallel_for** runs many work items. Use **sycl::range<1>(4)**. After **q.wait()**, print exactly:

```
parallel_for submitted
```

{hints: h.parallel_for(sycl::range<1>(4), [=](sycl::id<1> idx) {}); }

problems.title: Using the work-item index
problems.instructions:
Submit a **parallel_for** with **range<1>(3)**. In the kernel you do not need to use the index. After **q.wait()**, print exactly:

```
3 indices
```

{hints: Same as learn_004 but range<1>(3) and print "3 indices\n".}

problems.title: cpu_selector
problems.instructions:
Create a queue using **sycl::cpu_selector_v** instead of default_selector_v. After creating the queue, print exactly:

```
CPU queue
```

{hints: sycl::queue q(sycl::cpu_selector_v); }

problems.title: Query device vendor
problems.instructions:
Create a default queue. Get the device **vendor** with **get_info<sycl::info::device::vendor>()** and print it followed by a newline. Validation accepts any non-empty line.

Example:

```
Intel Corporation
```

{hints: q.get_device().get_info<sycl::info::device::vendor>() }

problems.title: Two single_tasks
problems.instructions:
Submit **two** **single_task** kernels (each can be empty). After **q.wait()**, print exactly:

```
Two single tasks
```

{hints: Call q.submit() twice with h.single_task([=]() {}); then q.wait(); }

problems.title: range<1> and range<2>
problems.instructions:
Submit a **parallel_for** with **sycl::range<2>(2, 3)** (2×3 = 6 work items). After **q.wait()**, print exactly:

```
2D range submitted
```

The kernel lambda can take **sycl::id<2> idx**; idx[0] and idx[1] are the indices.

{hints: h.parallel_for(sycl::range<2>(2, 3), [=](sycl::id<2> idx) {}); }

problems.title: Host and device
problems.instructions:
Print exactly two lines from the **host** (no kernel required):

```
Host runs first
Device runs later
```

Use two **printf** calls. This reinforces that host code runs on the CPU and device code runs on the selected accelerator.

{hints: printf("Host runs first\n"); printf("Device runs later\n"); }

tests.expected_stdout:
Host runs first
Device runs later

problems.title: Wait and print
problems.instructions:
Submit a **single_task** that does nothing. After **q.wait()**, print exactly:

```
Kernel finished
```

{hints: submit single_task, q.wait(), then printf.}

problems.title: 5 work items
problems.instructions:
Submit a **parallel_for** with **range<1>(5)**. After **q.wait()**, print exactly:

```
5 items
```

{hints: range<1>(5), then wait and printf.}

problems.title: Default selector then print
problems.instructions:
Create a queue with **default_selector_v**. Then print exactly:

```
Default device ready
```

{hints: sycl::queue q(sycl::default_selector_v); printf.}

problems.title: single_task then parallel_for
problems.instructions:
Submit first a **single_task**, then a **parallel_for** with **range<1>(1)**. After **q.wait()**, print exactly:

```
Both submitted
```

{hints: Two q.submit() calls, then q.wait(), printf.}

problems.title: SYCL Learn complete
problems.instructions:
Write a program that prints exactly:

```
SYCL LEARN complete!
```

Include `<sycl/sycl.hpp>` and `<cstdio>`. No queue or kernel required.

{hints: printf only.}

problems.title: Print the Device Name
problems.instructions:
Create a SYCL queue and print the **name** of the selected device. Use **q.get_device().get_info<sycl::info::device::name>()**. Output must be one non-empty line.

Example:

```
Intel(R) UHD Graphics 620
```

{hints: get_info returns std::string; use .c_str() for printf.}

problems.title: Add Two Numbers on the Host
problems.instructions:
Read two integers from stdin and print their sum. Include SYCL and create a queue; do read/sum/print on the host.

Input: 2 3

Output:

```
5
```

{hints: scanf or std::cin; printf or std::cout.}

problems.title: parallel_for with 8 Work Items
problems.instructions:
Submit a **parallel_for** with **range<1>(8)**. After **q.wait()**, print exactly:

```
8 work items done
```

{hints: range<1>(8), wait, printf.}

problems.title: Read and print one integer
problems.instructions:
Read one integer from stdin and print it. Create a queue (you may not use it). Output must be the number followed by a newline.

Input: 42

Output:

```
42
```

{hints: scanf("%d", &x); printf("%d\n", x); }

problems.title: Multiply two numbers on host
problems.instructions:
Read two integers and print their **product**. Create a queue; do I/O and multiplication on the host.

Input: 6 7

Output:

```
42
```

{hints: scanf two ints, printf product.}

problems.title: 10 work items
problems.instructions:
Submit a **parallel_for** with **range<1>(10)**. After **q.wait()**, print exactly:

```
10 items done
```

{hints: range<1>(10), wait, printf.}

problems.title: Three single_tasks
problems.instructions:
Submit **three** **single_task** kernels (each empty). After **q.wait()**, print exactly:

```
Three tasks done
```

{hints: Three q.submit() with h.single_task([=]() {}); }

problems.title: Sum of three integers
problems.instructions:
Read **three** integers from stdin and print their sum. Create a queue; do I/O on the host.

Input: 1 2 3

Output:

```
6
```

{hints: scanf three ints, printf sum.}

problems.title: range<2>(3, 4)
problems.instructions:
Submit a **parallel_for** with **sycl::range<2>(3, 4)** (12 work items). After **q.wait()**, print exactly:

```
3x4 done
```

{hints: h.parallel_for(sycl::range<2>(3, 4), [=](sycl::id<2> idx) {}); }

problems.title: Device max work group size
problems.instructions:
Create a queue and get **max_work_group_size** with **get_info<sycl::info::device::max_work_group_size>()**. Print that integer followed by a newline.

Example:

```
256
```

{hints: Returns size_t; use %zu or cast to long and use %ld for printf.}

problems.title: Two Kernels in Sequence
problems.instructions:
Submit **two** kernels: first a **single_task**, then a **parallel_for** with **range<1>(2)**. After **q.wait()**, print exactly:

```
Two kernels submitted
```

{hints: Two q.submit() calls, then q.wait(), printf.}

problems.title: single_task and two parallel_fors
problems.instructions:
Submit **three** kernels in order: one **single_task**, then **parallel_for(range<1>(2))**, then **parallel_for(range<1>(3))**. After **q.wait()**, print exactly:

```
1+2+3 items
```

{hints: Three submit() calls with the correct ranges.}

problems.title: CPU then default queue
problems.instructions:
Create a queue with **cpu_selector_v** and print exactly:

```
CPU
```

Then create a second queue with **default_selector_v** and print exactly:

```
Default
```

So the output is two lines: CPU, then Default.

{hints: Two queues, two printf calls.}

problems.title: Four parallel_fors
problems.instructions:
Submit **four** **parallel_for** kernels with **range<1>(1)**, **range<1>(2)**, **range<1>(3)**, **range<1>(4)**. After **q.wait()**, print exactly:

```
1+2+3+4
```

{hints: Four submit() calls with range<1>(1), range<1>(2), range<1>(3), range<1>(4).}

problems.title: 2D range 5x2
problems.instructions:
Submit a **parallel_for** with **sycl::range<2>(5, 2)** (10 work items). After **q.wait()**, print exactly:

```
5x2 submitted
```

{hints: range<2>(5, 2), kernel [=](sycl::id<2> idx) {}.}

problems.title: Buffer and host_accessor (read)
problems.instructions:
Create a **sycl::buffer<int, 1>** of size 1, initialized with the value **7**. In a **submit**, use a **single_task** to leave the buffer unchanged (or write 7). After **q.wait()**, create a **sycl::host_accessor** to read the buffer and print the single element followed by a newline.

Expected output:

```
7
```

{hints: buffer<int,1> buf(std::vector<int>{7}); in the kernel use accessor with read_write; after wait use host_accessor to read and printf.}

problems.title: Buffer: add one to each element
problems.instructions:
Create a **buffer** of 3 integers: **1, 2, 3**. In a **parallel_for(range<1>(3))**, each work item adds 1 to its element (so 2, 3, 4). After **q.wait()**, use a **host_accessor** to read the buffer and print the three values on one line: **2 3 4** followed by a newline.

Expected output:

```
2 3 4
```

{hints: accessor in kernel with idx[0]; each work item does acc[idx[0]] = acc[idx[0]] + 1; host_accessor to read and printf.}

problems.title: Sum array on device
problems.instructions:
Create a **buffer** of 4 integers: **10, 20, 30, 40**. Use a **single_task** to compute the sum (10+20+30+40 = 100) into a **second buffer** of size 1. Read the sum back with a **host_accessor** and print it followed by a newline.

Expected output:

```
100
```

{hints: Two buffers: one for input (size 4), one for output (size 1). In single_task use two accessors; read from first, write sum to second[0].}
